summary: I was born and raised in Birmingham, Alabama, where my family lived in modest circumstances. Birmingham was the backdrop of many pivotal civil rights events that shaped my youth, including the activism of Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth and others trying to desegregate schools like Phillips High School, where my sons later graduated. It was also the place where I first encountered the Freedom Riders and became involved in the movement. The city had two bus stations, Trailways and Greyhound, and I was more familiar with the Greyhound station because of my grandfather.

When I was about nine or ten years old, I stayed for a few years in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, with my great-aunt Hattie. I attended Gladstone Elementary and lived in Hazelwood on Sylvan Avenue. It was during this time that Emmett Till was murdered, and I remember the adults around me discussing it, which sparked my early awareness of racial violence. Pittsburgh was also where I learned different social norms from other children, like how to speak and order a 'pop' instead of a 'drink.'

I was about nine or ten years old when Emmett Till was brutally murdered, and although I didn't fully understand the gravity of lynching, I knew a black child had been killed. The adults in my great-aunt's home in Pittsburgh talked about it, and it piqued my curiosity. I even spent a nickel to buy a newspaper to try to understand what had happened, marking one of my first encounters with the harsh realities of racial violence.

When I was about fourteen, I followed the tragic case of Mack Parker in Poplarville, Mississippi. He was arrested on accusations similar to those against Emmett Till and was kept in jail under threat of violence. Eventually, he was taken from jail and murdered, a horrifying event that deeply affected me and coincided with the rising civil rights activities like the Montgomery bus boycott and school desegregation efforts.

Poplarville, Mississippi, was the place where Mack Parker was held in jail before he was murdered. This town became part of the painful history I followed as a young teenager witnessing racial violence and injustice. The events there were part of the larger context of racial tensions and civil rights struggles happening across the South during that time.

The Montgomery bus boycott was one of the major civil rights events I followed closely as a teenager. It happened shortly after Mack Parker's murder and was part of the wave of activism that inspired me. Hearing about the boycott and other actions made me eager to get involved in the movement for change.

The University of Mississippi was significant in the civil rights movement when Autherine Lucy attempted to integrate the school. This event was part of the broader struggle for desegregation that I witnessed growing up in Birmingham. It showed the challenges African Americans faced even in higher education during the late 1950s and early 1960s.

Autherine Lucy was a courageous figure who tried to enter the University of Mississippi, breaking racial barriers in education. Her efforts were part of the civil rights battles I was aware of as a teenager, alongside other events like the Montgomery bus boycott and local desegregation attempts in Birmingham. Her story inspired many in the movement.

Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth was a key civil rights leader in Birmingham whom I remember well. Along with other ministers like Charles Billups and C. Herbert Oliver, he worked tirelessly to desegregate schools, including Phillips High School where my children later graduated. His activism was a major influence in my community during the height of the civil rights movement.

Reverend Charles Billups was one of the ministers in Birmingham actively involved in efforts to desegregate schools during the civil rights era. I recall him working alongside Fred Shuttlesworth and others to challenge segregation in places like Phillips High School. Their leadership helped shape the movement in my hometown.

Reverend C. Herbert Oliver was another Birmingham minister who played a role in the civil rights struggle to desegregate schools. He worked with leaders like Fred Shuttlesworth and Charles Billups during a time when these efforts were met with fierce resistance. Their work was part of the environment that influenced my growing awareness and involvement.

Phillips High School in Birmingham was a focal point of desegregation efforts led by ministers like Fred Shuttlesworth. Both of my sons graduated from Phillips, so it holds personal significance for me. The school symbolized the challenges and progress of the civil rights movement in our community.

The sit-in movement was one of the exciting developments in the civil rights struggle that I followed as a teenager. It came after events like the Montgomery bus boycott and was part of the wave of direct action that energized young people like me. The sit-ins showed a new, bold way to confront segregation.

I was deeply inspired by the Freedom Riders when I was sixteen and wanted to join them. I bought a ticket from Birmingham to Montgomery to participate, but after learning about the principle of nonviolence from Wilson Brown, I realized I wasn't ready and got a refund. The Freedom Riders' courage and actions, including the bus burning in Anniston, Alabama, were pivotal moments that motivated me to get more involved in the movement.

Atlanta was the city where I attended my first Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) meeting after being invited by Wilson Brown. It was my first time visiting Atlanta, and I was eager to learn more about the civil rights movement and get involved. This trip marked the beginning of my deeper engagement with SNCC and direct activism.

Anniston, Alabama, was the site where the Freedom Riders' bus was attacked and burned. I remember hearing about this violent event as the Freedom Riders traveled through the South, which underscored the dangers they faced. This incident happened just before the riders came to Birmingham, where I was preparing to join them.

In Birmingham, there were two bus stations: Trailways and Greyhound. I was not very familiar with Trailways and was more drawn to the Greyhound station, partly because my grandfather often went there. Trailways was just part of the transportation landscape during the Freedom Rides, but it wasn't where I focused my attention.

Greyhound Lines was the bus station in Birmingham where I spent time during the Freedom Rides. I was more familiar with Greyhound because of my grandfather, who frequently went there. When the Freedom Riders arrived, the Greyhound station was roped off, and it became a focal point for the movement and my own involvement.

Ralph Abernathy was a prominent civil rights leader whose name I first heard through Candace, a young lady I knew from the projects. She told me he was her uncle, though at the time I didn't fully grasp his importance. Abernathy was part of the network of leaders supporting the Freedom Riders and the broader movement in Birmingham.

Wilson Brown was a man I met in Birmingham who was part of the welcoming committee for the Freedom Riders. He talked to me about the importance of nonviolence and explained that I couldn't join the Freedom Riders unless I understood and committed to that principle. His guidance was crucial in shaping my approach to activism and led me to eventually attend SNCC meetings.

The A.G. Gaston Motel in Birmingham was a special place where my friends and I would gather. It was also a spot where I often saw Wilson Brown, who invited me to get more involved in the civil rights movement. The motel served as a hub for planning and socializing among activists during the movement.

The Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) was the organization that marked my first serious step into civil rights activism. After meeting Wilson Brown, I attended my first SNCC meeting in Atlanta, eager to learn and participate. SNCC represented the youthful, direct-action wing of the movement that I wanted to be part of.

I first came into contact with the Nation of Islam during the time Mack Parker was in jail before his murder. Their newspaper, Muhammad Speaks, was the only one that consistently covered his case, which drew me to them initially. I attended some meetings but eventually felt their activities weren't as visibly active as the direct actions like sit-ins and Freedom Rides, so I moved on to other groups.

Muhammad Speaks was the Nation of Islam's newspaper that I noticed during Mack Parker's imprisonment and after his murder. It was the only paper that gave meaningful daily coverage of his case, which attracted my attention. This exposure was my first real contact with the Nation of Islam and their perspective on racial issues.

The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) played a significant role in the Freedom Rides, which I followed closely. While I was more involved with SNCC and local Birmingham activism, I recognized CORE's contributions to the movement, especially in organizing direct actions like the Freedom Rides. Their work was part of the broader civil rights struggle I was witnessing.

During my time in Pittsburgh as a child, I remember traveling through Cincinnati, where my aunt told me I could sit anywhere I wanted on the bus. This was a stark contrast to the segregated seating I experienced later in Birmingham. That experience in Cincinnati was part of my early exposure to different racial dynamics in the North.
